design_philosophy.core_loop: Select routes, negotiate airport access, purchase aircraft, manage service quality and fares, compete against AI/human opponents, resolve quarterly results affected by world events, repeat until connecting all 22 cities with profitable passenger targets
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Balancing expansion ambitions against geopolitical restrictions, competitor actions, random world events, and sustained profitability across multiple timeframes
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Build a global airline empire from scratch, managing every aspect of international aviation business while navigating real-world political and economic obstacles
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: The game enforces hard geopolitical simulation constraints (e.g., Moscow-based airlines restricted to Soviet aircraft, negotiation difficulties with opposing blocs during Cold War); win conditions demand both network coverage AND profitability, not just growth; world events materially shape strategy rather than serve as flavor

difficulty.continues_system: Bankruptcy with reorganization option after 4 consecutive quarters negative balance
difficulty.checkpointing: Quarterly turn-based structure with rolling financial assessment; 32-year game limit (128 turns) triggers automatic loss if win conditions unmet
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Explicit difficulty selection affects passenger volume, frequency/severity of world events, and win condition stringency (2.5–4.5 million passengers); headquarters city choice provides handicap options (New York/London/Tokyo start stronger; Lima/Nairobi/Honolulu start weaker); two timeframe options (1963–1995 vs 1983–2015) alter aircraft availability and geopolitical landscape

progression.upgrade_system: Aircraft purchases (constrained by geopolitical affiliation and budget), route expansion, service quality improvements, advertising campaigns, peripheral business investments (hotels, shuttle services), airport access negotiation, charter airline acquisition (51%+ ownership integration)
progression.unlock_mechanism: Routes unlock based on airport access negotiation and aircraft capability; Perestroika event removes Cold War geopolitical restrictions; passenger volume and profitability gates progression toward win condition (connect all 22 cities + threshold passengers while profitable)
replayability_hooks: Two distinct timeframes with different technological/geopolitical contexts
Multiple starting cities with varying resource levels and strategic implications
Random world events (labor strikes, Olympics, natural disasters) alter each playthrough
Multiplayer competitive modes (AI or human opponents)
Difficulty scaling affects event frequency and win thresholds

world_building_techniques: Historical timeframe anchors (1963–1995 / 1983–2015) contextualize aircraft and routes
Geopolitical event simulation (Perestroika, Cold War restrictions) embeds real-world history
World events (Olympics, labor strikes, natural disasters) create dynamic narrative texture
City selection and airline headquarters establish implicit geographic storytelling
Stock market and charter airline mechanics suggest competitive business landscape

faction_indicators: Soviet vs. Western geopolitical alignment (Moscow-based airlines restricted to Soviet equipment)
Starting city choice (major hubs like New York/London/Tokyo vs. peripheral cities like Lima/Nairobi)
Charter airline assimilation signals corporate dominance
danger_signals: Four consecutive quarters of negative balance triggers bankruptcy
Competitor control of key routes threatens profitability
Labor strikes delay aircraft shipments, disrupting expansion plans
Geopolitical events restrict negotiation options and aircraft availability
environmental_storytelling: 22-city network represents global aviation infrastructure of the era
Airport access negotiation reflects real-world slot allocation politics
World events (Olympics boost traffic) tie gameplay to historical moments
Aircraft technology progression (long-distance capability, supersonic competition in Scenario 2) reflects aviation history
